rows.label: statement
rows.note: Note: Irregular verbs do not take '-ed'. Learn the past simple form of irregular verbs.

content.subtitle: Use
items: Single completed actions: Lisa joined a yoga class and discovered she loved it.
Habits in the past: Did you play football every Saturday when you were a child?
Permanent situations in the past: A well-known cyclist trained on the roads near our town.
General truths and facts about the past: Tennis didn't become an Olympic sport again until 1988.
The main events in a story: The striker received the ball, turned sharply, and fired it into the net.

content.subtitle: Emphatic form
content.description: We can also use did in past simple statements for emphasis.
examples: I did score that goal. Ask anyone who was there!

content.subtitle: Use
items: Actions happening at a particular moment in the past: At three in the afternoon, Marcus was practising his tennis serve.
Temporary situations in the past: Sophie was training at the national athletics centre that summer.
Annoying past habits (usually with always): When we trained together, my teammate was always arriving late for practice.
Actions in progress over a period of time: Oliver was cycling through the mountains all weekend.
Two actions in progress at the same time: Were the coaches watching our game while we were competing in the relay?
Background information in a story: The crowd was cheering loudly and the players were warming up on the pitch.

content.subtitle: Be careful
content.description: When one action in the past interrupts another action in progress, we use the past simple and the past continuous together. I was jogging in the park when I suddenly twisted my ankle.
notes: We do not use the past continuous for regular or repeated actions in the past. ✓ When we were at summer camp, we went swimming every morning.
We do not usually use stative verbs in continuous tenses.

content.subtitle: Use
items: Situations and states before the past: We'd been members of the cycling club for a few months before we entered our first race.
Completed actions before a moment in the past: I'd already reserved a spot at the climbing wall when they announced the session was free.
Completed actions where the important thing is the result at a moment in the past: We didn't feel like going for a run because we had just finished a tough football match.

content.subtitle: Notes
content.description: There is often little or no difference in meaning between the past perfect simple and the past simple. After we have used the past perfect simple once, we often then use the past simple instead.
items: Phrases such as It was the first/second/etc time ... are followed by the past perfect simple: It was the first time I'd taken part in a triathlon.

content.subtitle: Use
items: Actions continuing up to a moment in the past: By the time the coach called us in, we had been training on the court for two hours straight.
Actions stopping just before a moment in the past: Danny was out of breath because he had been sprinting around the athletics track.

content.subtitle: Use
items: Past habits, particularly for the distant past: When I was little, my dad would take me to the local leisure centre every Sunday.

content.subtitle: Be careful
content.description: We don't often use would in questions or negative statements with this meaning. In negative statements, we can use would never.

rows.label: negative
rows.left: didn't use to / never used to / used not to

content.subtitle: Use
items: Past habits and states, particularly for the distant past: My uncle used to go cycling every weekend before he moved abroad.

content.subtitle: Be careful
content.description: To talk about a past state, we can use used to, but not would.
notes: ✓ We used to have a sports club membership that included the swimming pool.

content.subtitle: Use
items: A situation that is familiar or no longer strange: I didn't enjoy early morning training at first, but now I'm used to it.
To talk about the process of becoming familiar with something, we use get used to: I'm gradually getting used to running longer distances each week.

content.subtitle: Be careful
content.description: Don't confuse **be/get used to + -ing** with **used to + infinitive**.
notes: ✓ I'm used to training before breakfast. (= It's no longer strange for me.)
✓ I used to train before breakfast. (= I did this in the past, but I don't anymore.)

sections.title: A: Past simple vs. Past continuous
sections.instructions: Circle the correct word or phrase.

items.sentence: I **saw / was seeing** Nina for the first time at the tennis club open day.
Carlos **watched / was watching** the football highlights when his coach called.
During the summer break, we **went / were going** to the swimming pool almost every afternoon.
Marco **practised / was practising** his free throws every day until he could score them consistently.
The line was busy when I rang. Who did you **talk / were you talking** to?
The club **owned / was owning** a large indoor sports facility near the town centre.
I **did / was doing** my stretching routine as soon as I got back from training.
The ball flew towards the net and the goalkeeper **jumped / was jumping** to make the save.
When our coach first joined the club, he **worked / was working** as a part-time fitness instructor.
I **got / was getting** to the gym by seven every morning during the tournament week!
We **played / were playing** badminton in the sports hall when the fire alarm went off.
No, you're wrong. I **did pass / was passing** the fitness test. I have the certificate to prove it.

sections.title: B: Complete using the correct form
sections.instructions: Complete using the correct form of the verb in brackets.

items.sentence: Where (you / go) ...... when I spotted you cycling through the park this morning?
(you / enjoy) ...... the match last night?
When we shared the changing room, Lee (always / take) ...... my sports gear without asking. It was so irritating!
When I got to the sports shop, I realised (not / have) ...... my wallet with me.
When I was young, we (go) ...... cycling together every weekend.
Usain Bolt (become) ...... a legend after breaking the world record in Beijing.
When you called this morning, I (work) ...... out in the garage so I didn't hear my phone.
I (hear) ...... from my old swimming coach last week. She asked about you.
The runner (appear) ...... to be exhausted and slowly came to a halt.
We (throw) ...... a farewell party for the team captain last Friday.

sections.title: C: Past simple vs. Past perfect
sections.instructions: Circle the correct word or phrase.

items.sentence: I (1) **just left / had just left** for training when I bumped into an old friend.
she (2) **gave / had given** me some news about the club.
I (3) **read / had read** the message on my phone during the break.
it (4) **made / had made** me smile.
I almost (5) **got / had got** a warning from the teacher!
I (6) **had / had had** my first diving lesson last Thursday.
I (7) **was / had been** late for the session.
when I (8) **got / had got** there, I suddenly realised I (9) **left / had left** my swimming kit at home!
So I (10) **went / had gone** all the way back to get it.
when I (11) **got / had got** back to the pool, the session (12) **already begun / had already begun**.
I (13) **learned / had learned** how to do some basic dives by the end.
The last time I (14) **spoke / had spoken** to you, you (15) **talked / had talked** about switching to a different sport.
What (16) **did you decide / had you decided** in the end?

sections.title: D: Past perfect simple & continuous
sections.instructions: Complete using the correct form of the verb in brackets.

items.sentence: By the time he retired, the coach ...... (WRITE) numerous training manuals for young athletes.
We chose the sports complex for our camp because we ...... (STAY) there the previous year.
We ...... (WAIT) for nearly an hour when the sports bus finally pulled up.
She was completely exhausted because she ....... (RUN) at full pace for twenty minutes.
I ...... (SEE) the team play before, so I knew what tactics to expect.
By the time she won her first medal, her coach ...... (KNOW) her for over three years.
The gymnast ...... (GET) ready for the championship for over a year and finally the big day arrived.
Theo ...... (HAVE) tennis lessons for very long, so he wasn't confident with his backhand.
Kim ...... (EAT) at the new sports café before, so she wasn't sure what to order.
I ...... (LISTEN) to my pre-match playlist for a few minutes when my headphones suddenly stopped working.
My sister and I ...... hiking in the hills almost every weekend last year.
We ...... about our next training session when a text message arrived.
While the marathon leader crossed the finish line, the next runner .......
Ryan ...... squash before, so I showed him how to hold the racket.
I wasn't sure how the new coach would respond because I ...... him long.
She ...... at the sports centre long when she was promoted to head coach.
I wanted to congratulate my teammate, but she .......
When we arrived at the stadium, I realised I ...... my match tickets at home!

sections.title: F: Error correction (verb forms)
sections.instructions: Each of the sentences has an incorrect verb form. Rewrite it correctly.

items.sentence: There was paint on her shirt because she'd **painted** in the studio all afternoon.
I missed the start of the race because I **collect** my race number at the desk.
It was clear that the players **has trained** hard because they were exhausted after the session.
We had been cycling for three hours straight and I **was needing** a break.
When the shuttle **was arriving** at the venue, we almost missed it because we were warming up.
During the winter break, I **was eating** too much and **watching** too many sports replays on TV!
Rosa **was learning** all the rules of basketball by the time she was eight.
The coach **was owning** a private sports academy until she retired last year.

sections.title: G: Would, used to, be/get used to
sections.instructions: Circle the correct word or phrase.

items.sentence: When he was young, my uncle **would / used to** live close to the sports stadium.
I really can't **be / get** used to training at such an early hour.
Athletes **would / are used** to train without any of the modern technology available today.
There **would / used to** be a tennis court in the park, but they built over it.
It felt odd at first, but now I'm used to **play / playing** on a full-size pitch.
Didn't you **use to / be** used to play for the school athletics team?
Priya was **being / getting** used to the idea of competing at national level.
Coaches never **would / used to** place so much emphasis on sports nutrition in the past.

sections.title: H: Key word transformation
sections.instructions: Complete each second sentence using the word given, so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence. Write between two and five words in each gap.

items.sentence:
Fifty years ago, people walked much more than they do today. **would**
Fifty years ago, ...... than they do today.

My coach wouldn't allow us to miss training sessions when I was young. **used**
My coach ...... allow us to miss training sessions when I was young.

Did you know that Andy played tennis at county level? **to**
Did you know that Andy ...... tennis at county level?

Sending sports results to friends via apps is no longer unusual today. **got**
We ...... sports results to friends via apps.

When he was a professional swimmer, my uncle often used to get home quite late. **would**
When he was a professional swimmer, my uncle ...... quite late.

Do you think you could learn to live without a regular training schedule? **used**
Do you think you could ...... without a regular training schedule?

In the past, fans wouldn't go as far away to watch their team as they do today. **use**
In the past, fans ...... as far away to watch their team as they do today.

This sports club has far more members than it had ten years ago. **used**
This sports club ...... so many members.
items.answer: never used to have/used not to have

sections.title: I: Error correction (extra words)
sections.instructions: Find the extra word in each line. Click it.

items.sentence: Youth sport would used to be quite different from what it is today.
Amateur athletes didn't use not to have access to such advanced training facilities.
Today's players may complain about their training schedules, but athletes in the past would [to] train with very little equipment or coaching support.
Many young players had often been left their local clubs by their early teens and were signed by professional academies.
This meant that they have had little free time for training or practice, especially when they had been working long shifts all day.
Of course, they got themselves used to the tough conditions eventually, but it would meant that dedication to sport came at a real cost.
Today, we are got used to having leisure time for sports and hobbies, a privilege that people in earlier times rarely were had.

sections.title: J: Cloze
sections.instructions: Write one word in each gap.

items.sentence: Marco lay awake. He had (1) ...... preparing for the next day (2) ...... a long time and now he couldn't sleep.
His team (3) ...... playing the regional champions at basketball in the final and Marco was the starting point guard.
He (4) ...... feeling the pressure.
He turned over and thought about how he (5) ...... first learned to play basketball.
They (6) ...... go to the local courts every evening after school and his father (7) ...... to show him all the moves.
He hadn't enjoyed the strict training at first, but he soon (8) ...... used to it.
He had (9) ...... the youngest player ever to join the club!
He quickly got used to taking important shots and (10) ...... under pressure, but tomorrow was different.
(11) ...... used to have problems sleeping before a game, he thought to himself.
But then again, I didn't (12) ...... to be the starting player in the final.
